We are given a problem about ordering six solos: guitarist (G), keyboard player (K), percussionist (P), saxophonist (S), trumpeter (T), violinist (V). Each performs exactly one solo, so we have a permutation of these six in positions 1 through 6.

Restrictions:
1. The guitarist does not perform the fourth solo. So G ≠ 4.
2. The percussionist performs a solo at some time before the keyboard player does. So P < K.
3. The keyboard player performs a solo at some time after the violinist does and at some time before the guitarist does. So V < K < G.
4. The saxophonist performs a solo at some time after either the percussionist does or the trumpeter does, but not both. This means: (S > P and S ≤ T) OR (S > T and S ≤ P)? So if S is after P, then S > P. If S is not after T, then S ≤ T. Similarly, if S is after T, then S > T, and if not after P, then S ≤ P. So we have two cases:
Case A: P < S ≤ T.
Case B: T < S ≤ P.
But note: In case A, we have P < S and S ≤ T, so P < S ≤ T. In case B, T < S and S ≤ P, so T < S ≤ P. However, note that in case B, since S ≤ P and T < S, we have T < S ≤ P, so T < P. Actually it says S is after exactly one, so if S is after P, then S is before T (since not after T). So indeed P < S < T. Similarly, if S is after T, then S is before P, so T < S < P. So condition 4 is: (P < S < T) OR (T < S < P). So S is strictly between P and T.

(1) G ≠ 4.
